text:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is currently the major analytical technique in metabolomics. Thousands of metabolites in cells can be quantified in a short time based on their mass-to-charge ratio and retention time. Due to the rapid development of high-resolution and fast-scanning mass spectrometers, it is now feasible to perform high-throughput untargeted mass spectrometry with minimal or even no chromatography. Although chromatography can effectively improve analytical selectivity and accuracy, it is still necessary to retain chromatography prior to mass spectrometry when dealing with complex samples such as bio-extracts.

In this study, we demonstrate for the first time that even a soft ionization source such as ESI leads to considerable in-source fragmentation. Fragment ions can correspond to real endogenous metabolites. For example, nucleotide-triphosphates are frequently fragmented to their corresponding nucleotide-diphosphates in the source, and hexose-phosphates are fragmented into triose-phosphates. Using yeast intracellular extracts, we give 20 examples of source fragmentation products that can masquerade as the endogenous metabolites of interest and, therefore, lead to potential quantification inaccuracies. In any case, our study confirms the importance of good chromatographic resolution for the precise determination of many central metabolic compounds.
